title: Federal court approves Anthropic's $1.5B author settlement — largest AI copyright payout on record
subtitle: Judge Araceli Martinez-Olguin signed off Monday on the deal ending the class action over the roughly 500,000 pirated books Anthropic downloaded to train Claude.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: U.S. District Judge Araceli Martinez-Olguin, sitting in the Northern District of California, granted final approval on Monday to Anthropic's $1.5 billion settlement with a class of authors and publishers. The deal ends a class action brought last year over Anthropic's use of pirated books to train the Claude model family. The class covers an estimated 500,000 works, and the settlement pays out $3,000 per work to the rights holders. It is the largest publicly known settlement of a U.S. copyright case, and the largest single payout tied to AI training data anywhere.

The mechanics of the case matter more than the headline number. Judge William Alsup, who has since retired, had already ruled that training a language model on lawfully acquired text is fair use — a defense Anthropic won on the merits. What survived that ruling was a separate claim: Anthropic downloaded a portion of its training corpus from pirate repositories, including Library Genesis and Pirate Library Mirror, and kept them in what internal documents called a "central library." The settlement resolves that piece, not the fair-use question. Training is still fair use under Alsup's holding. Sourcing training data from piracy is not.

The precedent this sets is narrow but expensive. Every frontier lab that scraped or downloaded books rather than licensing them now has a price tag on the exposure: $3,000 per work. Multiply by the size of a modern training set and the numbers get large fast. The pending Suno music case, which expanded from 560 works to 61,026 specifically identified recordings, and the Hachette/Cengage/Elsevier suit against Google filed July 14 are the next tests. Anthropic's own $1.5 billion is now the anchor number both sides will negotiate against.

For learners: the AI industry just settled a question that mattered less than the one still open. "Can you train on copyrighted text?" is now, at least in one U.S. district, mostly yes. "Can you get the text however you want?" is now clearly no. If you are building anything that involves ingesting other people's work — for training, for retrieval, for anything — the sourcing chain is the legal risk, not the use. Keep receipts for how you got the data. That is the discipline that would have saved Anthropic $1.5 billion.
